---
title: "6 · Conclusions"
---

## What the evidence says, in one table

| Question | Technique | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Ether sent × transaction count | Pearson (log) + Kendall | Positive, weak–moderate: volume ≠ frequency |
| Avg value received × sent | Pearson (log, one-sided) + Kendall | Strong positive: characteristic value scale per address |
| Sending cadence × fraud | Welch's *t* + Mann–Whitney | Significant group difference: fraud has a rhythm |
| Ether outflow × sent-token type | Welch's ANOVA + Games–Howell | Significant token-ecosystem effect |
| Received-token type × fraud | χ² + Cramér's V | Significant association |
| Balance ~ activity + fraud | MLR + interaction | See Ch. 4 model comparison |
| Fraud ~ behaviour × token type | Logistic + interaction, ROC/AUC | See Ch. 5 model comparison |

Every hypothesis-testing verdict was confirmed by its alternative estimator —
none of the conclusions hinge on a distributional assumption.

## What it means for fraud analytics

The picture that emerges is *behavioural*: fraud-relevant signal lives not only in
how much value moves, but in **when** it moves (cadence), **how** it decomposes
(volume vs frequency), and **where** it moves (token ecosystems) — and the token
context plausibly *moderates* the behavioural signals rather than merely adding to
them. That is exactly the structure production fraud systems encode as engineered
features and feature crosses, which makes these inferential results a principled
feature-selection argument for predictive modelling.

## Limitations

Cross-sectional aggregates rather than transaction-level flows; a fraud label of
undocumented provenance; heavy-tailed variables analysed on log scales
(multiplicative interpretations); multiple hypotheses on one dataset (results
should be read as an integrated investigation, not independent discoveries);
in-sample model assessment — deployment-grade validation belongs to a
dedicated ML treatment; and two label-leakage artefacts (missing ERC20
aggregates and scam-airdrop token identities perfectly predicting the flag)
that make naive predictive claims on this dataset untrustworthy — documented
in Chapter 5.

## Where this goes next

The natural continuation is predictive: supervised learning with a proper held-out
protocol, class-imbalance handling, and cost-sensitive threshold selection — the
subject of a separate end-to-end project (phishing detection pipeline) in this
portfolio — and, on the data side, transaction-level (rather than aggregate)
features, where the analysis finding suggests the richest signal lives.
